did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning and deep learning techniques, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), ResNet50, and VGG16, to classify skin cancer (melanoma vs. benign lesions) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. It also employed GLCM for feature extraction and Otsu's method for segmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of machine learning models (CNN, ResNet50, VGG16) for skin cancer classification using the ISIC dataset. It details specific methodology including data collection (2400 images), pre-processing (Otsu's segmentation, GLCM feature extraction), and reports new results (accuracy metrics) in a conference proceeding (ICSSES 2023).

title: Classification of Skin Cancer using Machine Learning Techniques
journal: 2023 International Conference on Smart Systems for applications in Electrical Sciences (ICSSES)
brief_summary: This study proposes a machine learning framework for classifying skin cancer into four classes: benign, melanoma in-situ, invasive melanoma, and later stages. The researchers utilized the ISIC dataset, comprising 2400 dermoscopic images, which were processed using Otsu’s segmentation and GLCM feature extraction. Three classification models were evaluated: a custom CNN, ResNet50, and VGG16. The experimental results demonstrated that the ResNet50 model achieved the highest accuracy of 85%, outperforming the custom CNN (80%), VGG16 (74.8%), and GLCM+CNN (70%) approaches. The authors conclude that deep learning techniques, particularly ResNet50, offer efficient and accurate solutions for early melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four classes in the classification task; 800 images used from the ISIC dataset.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete classes: Benign, Melanoma in-situ, Invasive melanoma, and Later stage melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer stages using dermoscopic images. The target classes are explicitly defined as 'Benign', 'Melanoma 0(insitu)', 'Melanoma 1(invasive)', and 'Melanoma later stages'. The study evaluates models (CNN, ResNet50, VGG16) based on accuracy for these discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A global initiative dataset sponsored by the International Society for Digital Imaging of the Skin (ISDIS), containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the CNN, ResNet50, and VGG16 models. The original 2400 images were augmented (horizontal/vertical flip) to create a training set of approximately 3200 images.
use.testing: 500 images were reserved for testing the models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using accuracy metrics (e.g., ResNet50 85%, CNN 80%) without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC Archive.notes: The study utilized 2400 images from the ISIC dataset, split into training (augmented to ~3200 images) and testing (500 images) sets. The classification task involved four classes: Benign, Melanoma 0 (in-situ), Melanoma 1 (invasive), and Melanoma later stages. Resnet50 achieved the highest accuracy among the tested models.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50 and VGG16 are described as pre-trained models used for feature extraction and classification. The custom CNN was built from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels with 3 RGB channels (for VGG16). Specific input size for ResNet50 and Custom CNN not explicitly stated, though standard for these architectures.

ResNet50.layers: 50 layers total (48 convolutional layers, 2 pooling layers: average pooling and max pooling)
ResNet50.description: Contains fifty layers, in which 48 are convolutional layers and other two are average pooling and max pooling layers.

VGG16.composition: 13 convolutional layers, 5 max-pooling layers, 3 dense layers
VGG16.description: VGGnet is a CNN architecture that contains 16 layers that includes convolutional layers, pooling layers, dense and flatten layers.

Custom CNN.components: Convolutional layers (for feature extraction like texture, color), Pooling layers (for downsampling and parameter reduction), Dense layer (for classification operation)
Custom CNN.description: Contains convolutional layers which are used for extracting the features like texture, color, etc., from the images. The pooling layers reduce the number of parameters by performing downsampling. A dense layer is used for performing the classification operation.

GLCM+CNN.components: Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for feature extraction, CNN for classification
GLCM+CNN.description: GLCM is used for feature extraction to identify textures using statistical values (entropy, energy, correlation, homogeneity, contrast, dissimilarity). These features are then fed into a CNN classifier.

training_details.data_augmentation: Horizontal flip, vertical flip (applied to training set to increase size from 2400 to ~3200 images)

classification_or_output_head.classes: 4 classes: Benign, Melanoma 0 (in-situ), Melanoma 1 (invasive), Melanoma later stages
classification_or_output_head.output_layer: Dense layer (for fully connected classification operation)

preprocessing_techniques: Noise removal using filters (specifically Median filter for salt and pepper/impulse noise and Conservative filter for maintaining edges while removing noise)
Hair and artifact removal using the Dull Razor algorithm (morphological operations to create a hair mask)
Image segmentation using Otsu’s method to separate lesion from background
Data augmentation (horizontal flip, vertical flip) to increase training set size from 2400 to approximately 3200 images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set images to increase the dataset size from 2400 to approximately 3200 images.
Applied to the training set images to increase the dataset size from 2400 to approximately 3200 images.

conclusions: The ResNet50 model achieved the highest accuracy of 85% among the tested classifiers (CNN, VGG16, GLCM+CNN) for the four-class skin cancer classification task.
The custom CNN model achieved an accuracy of 80%, outperforming VGG16 (74.8%) and the hybrid GLCM+CNN approach (70%).
The study demonstrated that deep learning techniques, particularly ResNet50, offer efficient and accurate solutions for early melanoma detection.
The proposed methodology can be developed into a user-friendly application to save time in the early diagnosis of fatal skin lesions.
The authors acknowledge that the system's incorrect results can have life-threatening consequences, emphasizing the necessity for reliable early diagnosis tools.
The proposed methodology has potential for application on different varieties of datasets to improve model efficiency.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small dataset of 2400 images from the ISIC archive, with only 500 images reserved for testing, which may limit the generalizability of the results.

The paper reports only accuracy metrics (ResNet50: 85%, CNN: 80%, VGG16: 74.8%, GLCM+CNN: 70%) without providing sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or confusion matrices for the specific classes, making it difficult to assess performance on imbalanced classes or false positive/negative rates.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the differences in performance between the models.

The dataset split details are inconsistent; the text states 2400 images total with 800 for benign and 500 for testing, but also mentions augmentation to ~3200 training images, yet does not clarify the distribution of the remaining malignant classes (in-situ, invasive, later stages) in the training/test sets.
The paper acknowledges that incorrect results can have life-threatening consequences but does not discuss clinical validation or deployment risks in a real-world medical setting.
External validation was not performed; the model was only evaluated on the specific subset of the ISIC dataset used, raising concerns about bias towards that specific data distribution.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; it is unclear if the augmentation (horizontal/vertical flip) was applied before or after the train-test split, which could lead to optimistic performance estimates if images from the test set were augmented and thus present in the training set.

The study compares deep learning models against traditional methods (SVM, k-means) from literature rather than establishing a robust baseline within the same experimental setup for all compared methods.
